coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot boots the photocatalytic overall hydrogen peroxide production on triazine-based covalent organic framework
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Wenhui
Huang, Kai
Shao, Bin
Shang, Shuaishuai
Jia, Zhonghao
Zhao, Xiaoying
Hu, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic overall H2O2 production shows significances in green manufactory through converting solar energy into valuable chemicals under nature H2O and O2. Covalent organic frameworks (COFs) hold promises in photocatalysis but still face challenges in bandgap regulation for dual-pathway of simultaneous two-electron oxygen reduction (2e⁻ ORR) and water oxidation (2e⁻ WOR). Here, we report an S-scheme heterojunction catalyst (CQDs@COF) constructed by integrating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into triazine-ureidoimine-based TP-COF. The CQDs enhance visible-light absorption, narrow the bandgap, and improve hydrophilicity/O2 affinity, synergistically boosting H2O2 generation. DFT calculations and controlled experiments elucidated the electron transfer pathway from CQDs to TP-COF via the S-scheme heterojunction, enabling a successful overall H2O2 production. Remarkably, CQDs@COF-3.5 achieved an exceptional H2O2 yield of 1062 μmol g⁻¹ within 60 min under ambient conditions using pure water without any sacrificial agents. This work offers a novel strategy to enhance charge separation/migration in COFs for efficient photocatalysis, advancing the rational design of high-performance photocatalytic systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs@COF photocatalysts
S-scheme heterojunction
Photocatalytic H2O2 production
Charge separation efficiency
Hydrophilicity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Performance of photocatalytic overall H2O2 production
Mechanism of photocatalytic overall H2O2 production

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram for the synthesis of CQDs@COF; (b) FT-IR spectra of TP-COF, CQDs and CQDs@COF-3.5; (c) PXRD patterns of TP-COF and CQDs@COF-3.5; (d) A-A stacking simulation model of 4 layers TP-COF; (e) TEM image of CQDs@COF-3.5; (f) Binding energy of CQDs with TP-COF; (g) Binding energies of H2O with CQDs and CQDs@COF-3.5, and H2O-contact angles on TP-COF and CQDs@COF-3.5, respectively.

(a) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of CQDs, TP-COF and CQDs@COF-3.5; (b) Tac plots of CQDs, TP-COF and CQDs@COF-3.5; (c) Energy band diagrams of CQDs, TP-COF and CQDs@COF-3.5; (d) Transient photocurrent response diagrams of TP-COF and CQDs@COF-3.5; (e) Performance of TP-COF and CQDs@COF-x versus time for overall H2O2 production in pure water and air for 60 min; (f) Performance of TP-COF and CQDs@COF-x for overall H2O2 production in pure water and air for 60 min.

(a) Reactions of TP-COF and CQDs@COF-3.5 in the presence of various scavengers; (b) EPR curves of ·O2 - by DMPO; (c) Reactions of TP-COF and CQDs@COF-3.5 in air and nitrogen conditions; (d) Electron-hole distribution of CQDs@COF-3.5 in excited states (blue and yellow represent holes and electrons, respectively); (e) Mechanistic diagram of H2O2 generation by CQDs@COF-3.5 in air and pure water.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot boots the photocatalytic overall hydrogen peroxide production on triazine-based covalent organic framework

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Engineering and Industrial Catalysis, School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, 130 Meilong Road, Shanghai 200237, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Quzhou Innovation Institute for Chemical Engineering and Materials, Quzhou, Zhejiang 324000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Quzhou Innovation Institute for Chemical Engineering and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Quzhou Innovation Institute for Chemical Engineering and Materials, Quzhou, Zhejiang 324000, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Co-first authors: Wenhui Wang, Kai Huang.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic overall H2O2 production shows significances in green manufactory through converting solar energy into valuable chemicals under nature H2O and O2. Covalent organic frameworks (COFs) hold promises in photocatalysis but still face challenges in bandgap regulation for dual-pathway of simultaneous two-electron oxygen reduction (2e⁻ ORR) and water oxidation (2e⁻ WOR). Here, we report an S-scheme heterojunction catalyst (CQDs@COF) constructed by integrating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into triazine-ureidoimine-based TP-COF. The CQDs enhance visible-light absorption, narrow the bandgap, and improve hydrophilicity/O2 affinity, synergistically boosting H2O2 generation. DFT calculations and controlled experiments elucidated the electron transfer pathway from CQDs to TP-COF via the S-scheme heterojunction, enabling a successful overall H2O2 production. Remarkably, CQDs@COF-3.5 achieved an exceptional H2O2 yield of 1062 μmol g⁻¹ within 60 min under ambient conditions using pure water without any sacrificial agents. This work offers a novel strategy to enhance charge separation/migration in COFs for efficient photocatalysis, advancing the rational design of high-performance photocatalytic systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Photocatalytic overall hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) production is an emerging green technology that converts solar energy into chemicals using H2O and O2 as raw materials [1–3]. To efficiently carry out the two-electron oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and two-electron water oxidation reaction (WOR) simultaneously, the catalyst must straddle the redox potentials for both ORR and WOR (1–2), which brings significance and challenges in both fundamental researches and reality applications [4].
(1)
ORR :
O ₂ +
2
e
-
+
2
H
+
→
H ₂ O ₂ ,
E = 0 . 28 V
( vs .
Normal
hydrogen
electrode
( NHE ) ,
pH = 7 )
(2)
WOR :
2
H
2
O + 2
h
+
→
H
2
O
2
+ 2
H
+
,
E = 1 . 35 V
( vs .
NHE ,
pH = 7 )

Among various photocatalysts, covalent organic frameworks (COFs), constructed from organic building blocks into periodic networks through covalent bonds, have received extensive attentions [5–7]. Many strategies such as metal doping and functional group modification have been proposed to promote their characteristics of high specific surface area, designable excitons donor-acceptor (D-A) structure, and high light absorption capability, and hence the photocatalytic performance [8–10]. Li et al. [11] reported thiophene-based COFs (TT-COF and TD-COF) with a D-A structure for overall hydrogen peroxide production, where the thiophene was the primary photoreduction unit for ORR, while the benzene ring (linked to the thiophene by the imine bond) was the central photooxidation unit for WOR. Shen et al. [12] developed thioether-modified triazine-based COFs (TDB-COFs) for overall hydrogen peroxide production, where the introduction of the thioether moiety broadened the visible light absorption and narrowed the band gap for facilitating the sluggish WOR. In addition, Guo et al. [13]. designed three pyrene-based COFs by introducing different functional groups of F and OH into the COF skeleton to understand the H2O-wettability and the charge separation efficiency to the overall H2O2 photosynthesis. Although COFs have great potentials to accomplish the challenges by exploring new building blocks, most available single COF catalysts are difficult to regulate the band gaps for driving both ORR and WOR reactions, simultaneously.

Alternatively, the construction of heterojunctions can be a simple and effective strategy to modulate the band structure and facilitate both reactions [14–16]. Recently, Yu et al. [17] successfully synthesized S-scheme COF/In2S3 heterojunctions by an in-situ growth method, which facilitated the separation and utilization of photo excitons for the efficient photocatalytic overall H2O2 production. Tang et al. [18] constructed Bi4O5Br2/COF heterojunctions, which increased charge carrier separation efficiency and H2O2 photosynthetic activity. Zhang et al. [19] presented a novel TiO2/TD-COF S-scheme heterojunction that enhanced photocatalytic H2O2 production through efficient charge separation and transfer by ORR and WOR. Notably, quantum dots (QDs) have exhibited high performance in photocatalysis due to their quantum electronic effects [20]. With abundant density of functionalized edge sites, they would exhibit strong interaction with COFs to produce heterojunctions to enhance the intrinsic photocatalytic activity [21]. Moreover, desirable QDs would modulate the hydrophobic surface of COFs to enhance the affinity to water, and further to facilitate the WOR pathway. However, owing to the complexity and diversity of both COFs and QDs, the mechanism for engineering QD/COFs heterojunctions to promote the photocatalytic overall H2O2 production is far from fully understood [22].

Herein, we fabricated a two-dimensional (2D) ureidoimine-conjugated triazine COF (TP-COF) as the substrate to construct an S-scheme type of heterojunction photocatalysts of CQDs@COFs for the overall H2O2 production. By dispersing the as-synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on TP-COF through a one-pot approach ( Fig. 1a), the surface amino and carboxyl groups on the CQDs manifested strong interactions with TP-COF, increasing the H2O-wettability on CQDs@COF, and facilitating the excitons-separation and charge transfer through the heterojunction. After the composition optimization, CQDs@COF-3.5 achieved an excellent H2O2 yield through dual-channels of ORR and WOR pathways. Combining the DFT calculations and the controlled experiments with scavengers, a deep insight into the S-scheme heterojunction mechanism governing CQDs@COF for its photocatalytic overall H2O2 production was illuminated. Accordingly, the rational design of heterojunction composites through regulating synergistic effects could be a promising way for boosting the photocatalytic H2O2 production.

All chemicals and reagents were commercially available and used without further purification. 2,4,6-Tris(4-formylphenyl)-1,3,5-triazine (TFPT) and 1,1′-(1,4-phenylene)diurea (PBU) were purchased from Shanghai Tengqian Bio-technology Co., Ltd. 1,4-dioxane, 1,3,5 -trimethylbenzene, and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were provided by Shanghai Meiruier Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Anhydrous citric acid, urea, disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), potassium iodide (KI), and potassium hydrogen phthalate were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyridine-N-oxide (DMPO), tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone, methanol, glacial acetic acid (Hac), and silver nitrate (AgNO3) were supplied by Sinopharm Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. The above reagents and solvents were of analytical grade.

Carbon quantum dots were obtained with a slight modification of the reported method [23]. Briefly, 0.5 g of anhydrous citric acid and 0.5 g of urea were ultrasonically dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water, which was transferred to a stainless steel Teflon-lined vessel and hydrothermally treated at 160 °C for 4 h. The black-green color solution obtained in the vessel was naturally cooled down to room temperature and then precipitated with 30 mL of acetone, which was vacuum-dried to obtain the CQDs.

TP-COF was constructed by the Schiff base reaction [24]. Specifically, 0.04 mmol of 2,4,6-tris(4-formylphenyl)-1,3,5-triazine (TFPT) and 0.06 mmol of 1,1′-(1,4-phenylene)diurea (PBU) were sequentially loaded into Pyrex tubes, and the mixtures were dispersed in a heat resistant glass tube using 1,4-dioxane (0.5 mL) and 1,3,5 -trimethylbenzene (0.5 mL) as solvents, and the resulting mixture was sonicated for 5 min to make it uniformly dispersed in a heat resistant glass tube. Finally, aqueous acetic acid solution (6 M, 0.1 mL) was added and sonicated for 1 min. Afterward, the heat resistant glass tubes were flame-sealed after three cryopump-thaw degassing cycles. The sealed tubes were placed in a blast drying oven at 120 °C for 3 days. At the end of the reaction, the precipitate in the Pyrex tubes was separated by filtration and thoroughly rinsed with tetrahydrofuran, acetone, and methanol, respectively. The resulting product was dried in a vacuum oven at 100 °C overnight to obtain the final yellow powder TP-COF in about 70 % yield.

The preparation procedure of CQDs@COFs was similar to that of TP-COF. 0.04 mmol of 2,4,6-tris(4-formylphenyl)-1,3,5-triazine (TFPT), 0.06 mmol of 1,1′-(1,4-phenylene)diurea (PBU), and a certain amount (x) of CQDs (2.5 mg, 3.5 mg, and 4.5 mg) were loaded into Pyrex tubes sequentially, and the CQDs were extracted using 1,4-dioxane (Dio; 0.5 mL) and 1,3,5-trimethylbenzene (Mesi; 0.5 mL) as solvents and aqueous acetic acid (6 M, 0.1 mL) as catalyst, the next procedure was the same as that of TP-COF, and the final brown powder CQDs@COF-x was obtained (x denotes the mass of added CQDs).

Under the dry air environment, 5 mg of photocatalyst was ultrasonically dispersed into 50 mL of deionized water, and then the dispersion was stirred in the dark for 30 min to ensure uniform dispersion and adsorption equilibrium. A 300 W xenon lamp was selected as the light source, and a 420 nm filter was chosen to simulate the photocatalytic reaction under visible light irradiation conditions. During the reaction, 0.5 mL of the sample was taken from the mixture at regular intervals, and the photocatalyst was filtered out using a 0.22 µm microporous filter membrane, then, deionized water (2.5 mL), potassium hydrogen phthalate solution (C8H5KO4, 0.1 M, 1 mL) and potassium iodide solution (KI, 0.4 M, 1 mL) were added sequentially and left for 30 min. In the presence of H+, H2O2 reacted with I- to form I3 - (3) and exhibited a characteristic absorption peak signal at 350 nm [25]. The absorbance of the mixture at 350 nm was measured by UV-Vis spectrophotometer and the concentration of H2O2 was calculated from the standard curve.
(3)
H
2
O
2
+ 3
I
−
+ 2
H
+
→
I
3
−
+ 2
H 2
O

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the different samples were obtained using a Bruker D8 Advance powder diffractometer, selecting Cu Kα as the radiation source (40 kV, 40 mA) at a scanning rate of 0.02° s−1. The microscopic morphology of the samples was observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200X). The N2 isotherm and specific surface area at 77 K were obtained by determining the N2 isotherm and specific surface area on an ASAP 2020 of Micromeritics, USA. The elemental and chemical states of the samples were determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo-Fisher). FT-IR spectra of the samples were obtained using a Thermo Fisher infrared spectrometer (Nicolet iS10, USA). Optical absorption spectra were collected with a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV-Vis spectrometer. The UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV-Vis-DRS) in the range of 200–800 nm were measured using a Shimadzu UV-3600 from Japan. The H2O-contacts angle of the samples were measured using Lauda Scientific LSA100, Germany. EPR experiments were performed on an electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrometer (Bruker EMXplus-6/1) using 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as the trapping agent. The electrochemical impedance (EIS), photocurrent response (TPC), and Mott-Schottky curves of the catalysts were measured on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E) using a typical three-electrode system (platinum electrode, working electrode, and saturated mercury electrode) with the working electrode being an FTO conductive glass coated with a thin film of the photocatalysts and the electrolyte being a 0.5 mol L−1 Na2SO4 solution.

A series of reactive substances are produced in water during the photocatalytic production of H2O2 via the ORR and WOR pathways. To better identify the main reactive substances in the photocatalytic reaction process, capture experiments of the reactive substances were conducted. Silver nitrate (AgNO3), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), and tert-butanol (TBA) were chosen in this study to quench the electron (e-), superoxide radical (·O2 -) and hydroxyl radical (·OH), respectively. The specific experimental steps were the same as the photocatalytic activity experiments. The only difference is that a certain amount of scavengers was added to the reaction system before dark treatment.

The Binding energy and molecular orbitals of CQDs@COF were calculated based on density functional theory (DFT) using the Gaussian 09 software package [26] The geometry optimization was carried out using B3LYP functional and 6–31g+ (d,p) basis sets [27,28] (Table S1). The electron-hole distribution of CQDs@COF-3.5 in excited states was calculated using Multiwfn 3.8 software [29,30].The binding energies (BEs) between the fragments of CQD and TP-COF, as well as between H2O molecule and the fragment of CQD or TP-COF were calculated, respectively, as (4).
(4)
B E =
E
n / m
−
E n
−
E m
Where E n/m, E n, and E m are the potential energy between two substances, and that of the corresponding pure substances, respectively, which are all in eV.

The precursor of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was firstly prepared by hydrothermal method. Its Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum shows characteristic peaks of amino group at 1080 cm−1 and carboxyl group at 1700 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 1b) [31,32] Meanwhile, the dynamic light scattering (DLS) plot suggests the dominant particle size of CQDs is about 2–7 nm (Figure S1). After a one-pot synthesis by mixing the as-synthesized CQDs with 2,4,6-tris(4-formylphenyl)-1,3,5-triazine (TFPT) and 1,1′-(1,4-phenylene)diurea (PBU), the CQDs@COF-x composites were constructed by varying the contents of CQDs (Fig. 1a). In comparison with the FT-IR spectra of building blocks of TFPT and PBU, the disappearance of stretching peaks of the aldehyde group at 1700 cm−1 and the amino group at 771 cm−1 [33], together with the appearance of a significant characteristic peak of CN stretching vibration at 1621 cm−1 [34] on the spectra of TP-COF (Figure S2) demonstrate the formation of the imine bond through the Schiff base condensation reaction, which acts as the linkage to prop the framework structure of TP-COF. Meanwhile, the characteristic peaks at 1500 cm−1, 1360 cm−1, and 800 cm−1 of the triazine ring further indicate the successful preparation of triazine-based COFs [35]. In addition, in comparison with the spectrum of this pristine TP-COF, the extra characteristic peaks of amino group and carboxyl group attributed to the CQDs, appear in the spectrum of CQDs@COF-3.5, suggesting the successful incorporation of the CQDs (Fig. 1b).

The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) pattern of the pristine TP-COF shows a quite good crystallinity with a main diffraction peak at 2.87°, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane. Meanwhile, three weak diffraction peaks appearing at 4.92°, 5.68°, and 7.49° are attributed to the (110), (200) and (210) crystal planes, respectively (Fig. 1c red curve). The simulated XRD pattern (black curve) using the P6/m space group with the A-A stacking mode is perfectly consistent with the experimental one (Rwp = 6.63 %, Rp = 4.81 %). Accordingly, the lattice parameters were determined to be a = b = 36.53 Å, c = 3.51 Å, α = β = 90°, γ = 120° (Fig. 1d). In comparison, when CQDs were incorporated, CQDs@COF-3.5 shows almost no changed PXRD spectra (blue curve), indicating the well-preserved crystal structure of TP-COF.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images further demonstrate the 2D nanosheet morphology of TP-COF (Figure S3), where CQD particles with the size of about 2 nm are highly distributed on CQDs@COF-3.5 (Fig. 1e). The binding energy (BE) between TP-COF and CQDs was calculated, which turns out to be as high as −1.30 eV (Fig. 1f), primarily originated from the synergistic effects of non-covalent interactions, specifically, the hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions. Polar functional groups (e.g., -COOH and -NH2) on the CQD surface can form hydrogen bonds with TP-COF. Furthermore, the pronounced surface charge disparity between CQDs and TP-COF induced strong electrostatic binding, which significantly enhanced their interfacial BEs (Figure S4). This incorporation of CQDs causes a significant reduction of specific surface area (S BET) in CQDs@COF-3.5, from 170 m² g-¹ of the pristine TP-COF to 56 m² g-¹ . Where the pore size distribution curves suggest the pores of TP-COF with 2.5 and 4 nm are almost completely blocked (Figure S5). Notably, the H2O-contact angle tests demonstrate a significantly improved hydrophilicity after incorporation of CQDs, that the contact angles of CQDs@COF-3.5 decrease from 65° on TP-COF to 27° (Fig. 1g). Consistently, DFT calculations further verified that the absolute value of BE of H2O with CQDs@COF is 0.63 eV, higher than that of 0.51 eV with TP-COF (Figure S6), owing to the great contributions of the CQDs with surface-exposed amino and carboxyl hydrophilic groups. This ensured the hydrophilic CQDs@COF, which can facilitate accessibilities of H2O. More importantly, not only the adsorption but also the activation of H2O molecules would make a critical contribution for achieving efficient WOR. Additionally, the formed hydrogen-bonding network from bulk to the CQDs@COF catalyst surface would facilitate the rapid proton extraction and transfer, thereby, favoring the consequent overall photocatalytic H2O2 production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of photocatalytic overall H2O2 production
ce:section.ce:para: The UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectra revealed TP-COF of broader visible absorption range owing to the combination of the triazine group with the ureidoimine group [36]. After the incorporation of CQDs, an expanded visible light trapping capability of CQDs@COF-3.5 was obtained with the visible light absorption to 800 nm due to the good light absorption properties of the CQDs ( Fig. 2a). Evaluated by the Tauc plots that energy band gap of CQDs@COF-3.5 decreases from 2.33 eV of TP-COF to much smaller of 2.18 eV. (Fig. 2b). Therefore, the introduction of CQDs not only broadens the visible absorption capability of TP-COF but also narrows the band gap for exciting the electrons. Meanwhile, their Mott-Schottky plots show positive slopes, suggesting the n-type semiconductor characteristics [37] (Figure S7). More significantly, the conduction band (CB) and the corresponding valence band (VB) of TP-COF are determined to be −0.80 V and 1.51 V, whereas −0.26 V and 1.84 V for CQDs (relative to the Normal hydrogen electrode (NHE)), respectively (Fig. 2c) [38]. Although CQDs possess the best light absorption capability and the narrowest band gap, its less negative CB potential than that of O2/ H2O2 redox potential of −0.33 V makes the WOR pathway to be the only approach for the H2O2 production. By loading CQDs on TP-COF, the CB and VB of CQDs@COF-3.5 was optimized as −0.61 V and 1.57 V, with more negative CB potential than that of the O2/ H2O2 for the ORR pathway, and more positive VB potential than the H2O/H2O2 redox potential (1.35 V) in the WOR pathway. Therefore, CQDs@COF-3.5 shows more thermodynamic favorable for the overall H2O2 production [39].

In addition, the dynamic photoelectronic properties were further illustrated by the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and the transient photocurrent (TPC). The radius of the arc of the EIS plot of CQDs@COF-3.5 is significantly smaller than that of TP-COF, further indicating the addition of highly conductive CQDs can significantly reduce the resistance of the electron migration and promote the separation of the photogenerated excitons due to the smaller band gap (Figure S8) [40]. Consequently, CQDs@COF-3.5 exhibits a stronger photocurrent response, with the current density of 2.5 times higher than that of TP-COF under the same conditions (Fig. 2d).

Owing to both excellent thermodynamic photoelectronic properties of CQDs@COFs, the photocatalytic performance of overall H2O2 production were conducted under simulated visible light irradiation with a 300 W Xenon lamp (λ > 420 nm) using pure water and air without adding any sacrificial agent (Figure S9). By increasing the amount of incorporated CQDs, the H2O2 yield within 60 min increases from 558 μmol g−1 on TP-COF to 853 μmol g−1 on CQDs@COF-2.5, and achieves twice amount of 1062 μmol g−1 on CQDs@COF-3.5. However, further adding more CQDs results in a decreased H2O2 yield of 726 μmol g−1 on CQDs@COF-4.5, suggesting too many CQDs with less negative potential of CB may reduce the contribution of ORR pathway on the overall H2O2 production (Figs. 2e and 2f). It is well known that the photocatalytic reactions usually observe the zero-order reaction [41]. Accordingly, fitted by the zero-order rate law, the dynamic H2O2 production rate curves reveal that CQDs@COF-3.5 exhibits the largest rate constant k of 9.98 μmol L−1 min−1, almost 1.5 times higher than that of TP-COF, consistent with the TPC results (Figure S10). It is worth to mentioning, comparing the initial H2O2 production rates before 10 min, which depends much on the prerequired adsorptions of H2O and O2, respectively [42]. The DFT calculations indicated that the bonding energy of O2 on TP-COF (-1.06 eV) was approximately twice as strong as that on CQDs (-0.43 eV) (Figure S11). This finding was further corroborated by the optimized O2 adsorption configurations on TP-COF and CQDs (Figure S12). For the TP-COF, O atoms maintain average closer proximity to the H atoms in the -NH-CO-NH- moiety, facilitating stronger O2 adsorption. In addition, Mulliken charge analysis demonstrated that O2 acquires a more electron transfer (-0.47 |e|) when adsorbed on TP-COF compared to CQDs (-0.23 |e|), which further proves the strong adsorption of O2 on TP-COF. More significantly, when loading CQDs on TP-COF, the CQDs@COF showed even enhanced O2 affinity (-1.92 eV). As there were strong interaction between CQDs and TP-COF through electrons transfer from CQDs to TP-COF, which made TP-COF in CQDs@COF richer in electrons and thus may fill the empty antibond π* orbitals of O2 to form stronger adsorbed state of ·O2 -. The incorporating CQDs not only enhances the overall H2O2 production rate through promoting the electron separation and transfer rates, but also significantly boosting the initial H2O2 production rate through improving the surface affinities with H2O and O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of photocatalytic overall H2O2 production
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the contribution of CQDs as well as the mechanism of the photocatalytic overall H2O2 production, we carried out a series of controlled experiments. First of all, almost no H2O2 was detected under dark conditions on both TP-COF and CQDs@COF-3.5, confirming the photocatalytic characteristics of the H2O2 production (Figure S13). Furthermore, under a pure O2 atmosphere, TP-COF and CQDs@COF-3.5 showed enhanced photocatalytic H2O2 production, achieving 743 μmol g⁻¹ and 1380 μmol g⁻¹ within 60 min, respectively (Figure S14). Accordingly, increasing the concentration of dissolved O2 have an important impact on H2O2 generation in the photocatalytic system. Evidently, with applying the electrons’ scavenger of silver nitrate (AgNO3, 0.1 mM), the H2O2 production on both TP-COF and CQDs@COF-3.5 are significantly reduced. This provides clear evidence that the photogenerated electrons and hence the ORR pathway plays a key driving role in the H2O2 production ( Fig. 3a). Notably, with the absence of the ORR pathway, TP-COF and CQDs@COF-3.5 show similar H2O2 production yield, suggesting their similar capability for the WOR pathway. Consistently, this can be attributed to their similar VB potentials of 1.51 V for TP-COF and 1.57 V for CQDs@COF-3.5, respectively. To further understand the contribution of ORR pathway, the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was performed to illustrate the key intermediate radicals. By using DMPO (5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide) as a spin trapping agent, the typical signals of ·O2 - were observed on TP-COF and CQDs@COF-3.5, respectively (Fig. 3b). This confirms the existence of the key intermediate ·O2 - radicals and hence the two-step 2e- ORR pathway (O2 → ·O2 - → H2O2) for the photocatalytic O2 reduction to produce H2O2. As expected, when adding p-benzoquinone (p-BQ, 0.5 mM) as a superoxide radical (·O2 -) scavenger, the H2O2 productions are significantly inhibited, further indicating the formation of ·O2 - is response for the H2O2 production through the two-step 2e- ORR. When 10 % tert-butanol (TBA) was added as a hydroxyl radical (·OH) scavenger, the photocatalytic H2O2 production within 60 min are extraordinarily increased by 301 μmol g−1 and 360 μmol g−1 for TP-COF and CQDs@COF-3.5, respectively. This can be attributed to the scavenging effect of TBA that the close combination with ·OH successfully prevents it from attacking H2O2, thus reducing the decomposition loss of H2O2 [43].

Furthermore, to quantitively evaluate the contributions of WOR and ORR pathways to the overall H2O2 production, we replaced the air flow by using a N2 flow (Fig. 3c). By passing through N2 for about 30 min to make 50 mL of H2O saturated, the photocatalytic H2O2 yield on TP-COF and CQDs@COF-3.5 decrease to 337 μmol g−1 and 543 μmol g−1, respectively. These provide clear evidence of the existence of the WOR pathway for the H2O2 production. Meanwhile, under N2 atmosphere without any contributions of ORR pathway, the significant reductions of H2O2 yield of 221 μmol g−1 and 519 μmol g−1 on TP-COF and CQDs@COF-3.5, revealing the ORR pathway contributed about a half (49 %) of H2O2 yield on CQDs@COF-3.5, whereas about 40 % on TP-COF, respectively.

The DFT calculations further provided the evidence that the electron mainly distribute at CQDs. The electrons distribution in CQDs@COF-3.5 indicates that the HOMO and LOMO orbital distributions are predominantly ascribed to TP-COF and CQDs, respectively (Figure S15). After the excitation, the easily excited electrons in CQDs migrate to the closely contact TP-COF, providing holes left in CQDs and excessive electrons in TP-COF. This is a typical electron transfer configuration of an S-scheme heterojunction between CQDs and TP-COF (Fig. 3d). In line with this, the high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectra of CQDs@COF-3.5 shifted to the higher BE in compared with TP-COF, confirming the synergistic interaction and electrons transfer between CQDs and TP-COF (Figure S16).

Accordingly, the possible mechanism for this photocatalytic H2O2 production on CQDs@COF-3.5 is elucidated (Fig. 3e) that under visible light irradiation, the electrons in CQDs with small band gap are easily excited, which transfer to TP-COFs and combined with the holes on its VB. As such, more negative potential of CB and more positive potential of VB facilitate the overall H2O2 production. Consequently, the photogenerated holes on the CQDs oxidize H2O to H2O2 by direct two-electron oxidation reaction and produce H+ at the same time for the ORR. Meanwhile, the adsorbed O2 on TP-COF acquired electrons to be reduced into H2O2 by a two-step 2e- ORR reaction in the presence of H+. Under the synergistic contributions of WOR and ORR pathways, the CQDs@COF-3.5 achieves excellent performance in the photocatalytic H2O2 production, superior to most reported COF-based photocatalytic H2O2 production to the best of our knowledge (Table S2).

In summary, the S-scheme heterojunction CQDs@COF photocatalysts for overall H2O2 production were successfully developed by dispersing the CQDs into ureidoimine-conjugated triazine TP-COF sheets. The incorporated CQDs not only improved the visible light absorption capability, narrowed the bandgap to enhance the photoelectronic properties of CQDs@COF-3.5, but also improve the hydrophilicity and O2-affinity to facilitate the photocatalytic overall H2O2 production. Combining with the DFT calculations and controlled tests, it was revealed that the electrons can be easily excited on the CQDs and effectively transfer to TP-COFs through the S-scheme heterojunction. Accordingly, a two-step 2e- ORR pathway occurred on the more negative CB and a one-step 2e- WOR pathway on the more positive potential of VB, simultaneously boosting the photocatalytic overall H2O2 production. Consequently, CQDs@COF-3.5 achieved an excellent photocatalytic H2O2 production, with a yield up to 1062 μmol g−1 in 60 min only using pure water in air without adding any sacrificial agent. Therefore, this study provides a new and convenient strategy to improve the charge separation and migration efficiency of COFs for the efficient photocatalysis.

Wenhui Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization. Bin Shao: Methodology. Kai Huang: Software. Shuaishuai Shang: Conceptualization. Xiaoying Zhao: Methodology. Zhonghao Jia: Formal analysis. Jun Hu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22250005, 22408095 and 22278126), China National Postdoctoral Program for Innovative Talents (BX20240116), and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2023M741170).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.cattod.2025.115399
